name: Material Mystery Boxes: Identify by Properties
description: Place 8-10 different materials inside opaque boxes or bags (wood block, metal spoon, plastic bottle cap, fabric swatch, rubber ball, glass marble, stone, sponge, aluminum foil, sandpaper). Without looking, reach in and identify each material by touch alone. Describe properties you feel: hard or soft? Rough or smooth? Heavy or light? Warm or cold? Flexible or rigid? After guessing, reveal and verify. Create a property chart listing each material and its tactile properties. Discuss which properties were most useful for identification and which materials were hardest to distinguish.
learning_objectives: Identify materials by tactile properties without visual cues
Describe material properties using correct vocabulary (hard, soft, rough, smooth, flexible, rigid)
Record observations systematically in a property chart
Understand that materials have identifiable, consistent characteristics
variations: Foundation (PreK-K): Use only 5 very different materials with simple hard/soft, rough/smooth sorting
Middle School: Add materials that are harder to distinguish (different plastics, different metals) and require more refined property descriptions

name: Strength Testing: Which Material Holds the Most Weight?
description: Test 5 common materials (paper, cardboard, aluminum foil, balsa wood, fabric) for strength in three modes. Tension: cut strips of each material to the same size, hang weights from the bottom until they tear, and record the breaking weight. Compression: roll or fold each material into a column, stack weights on top until it buckles, and record the failure weight. Bending: support each strip at two points 20 centimeters apart, add weight at the center until it fails, and record the load. Create a complete materials comparison chart with all three modes. Rank materials by each property. Discuss: is any material best at everything? Why not? What does this mean for material selection?
learning_objectives: Measure material strength using controlled, repeatable tests in three loading modes
Organize test data in a structured comparison chart
Understand that materials perform differently under tension, compression, and bending
Recognize that no single material is best at everything -- selection involves tradeoffs
variations: Foundation (K-2): Sort materials into strong/weak and bendy/stiff categories by feel without measurement
High School: Calculate stress (force per area) and strain (deformation per length) to create stress-strain curves

name: Materials Scavenger Hunt: Find All Five Families
description: Search your home, school, or neighborhood to find at least two examples of each of the five material families: metals (steel, aluminum, copper), polymers/plastics (bottles, bags, toys), ceramics (plates, bricks, glass), composites (fiberglass, plywood, reinforced concrete), and natural materials (wood furniture, cotton clothing, leather shoes, stone walls). For each item found, record: what the object is, which material family it belongs to, what key properties it has (why can you tell it is metal, plastic, etc.), and why you think that material was chosen for that object. Present findings as a poster, table, or slideshow showing all five families represented.
materials: recording sheet with columns for object, family, properties, and reasoning
learning_objectives: Identify examples of all five material families in real environments
Classify objects into material families based on observable properties
Explain why specific materials were chosen for specific applications
Understand that material families are everywhere in daily life
variations: Foundation (PreK-K): Find one thing made of metal, one of plastic, one of wood -- sort three families with guidance
Middle School: Find examples where the same object uses multiple material families (car: metal body, plastic interior, glass windows, rubber tires, fabric seats) and explain each choice

name: Plastic Classification Challenge
description: Collect 10 different plastic objects from around your home (water bottle, milk jug, yogurt container, plastic bag, foam cup, food container, shampoo bottle, toy, blister packaging, credit card or gift card). Examine each for the recycling number (1-7) stamped on the bottom. Record the number and the plastic type it represents (1=PET, 2=HDPE, 3=PVC, 4=LDPE, 5=PP, 6=PS, 7=Other). Test each with simple tests: does it float or sink in water? Is it rigid or flexible? Is it transparent or opaque? Can you scratch it with your fingernail? Create a classification chart showing how different types of plastic have different properties. Research which types your local recycling facility actually accepts. Discuss: if all plastics are different, why do we call them all plastic?
learning_objectives: Identify different types of plastic using recycling codes and property tests
Understand that 'plastic' is not one material but many with different properties
Classify plastics by measurable characteristics (density, flexibility, transparency)
Evaluate which plastics are actually recyclable in practice versus in theory
variations: Elementary: Sort plastics into rigid vs. flexible and transparent vs. opaque without recycling codes
High School: Research the polymer chemistry of each plastic type and explain how molecular structure determines properties

name: Build a Joint: Glue vs. Tape vs. Screw vs. Tie
description: Join two identical pieces of wood (popsicle sticks or small boards) using four different joining methods: wood glue, duct tape, a screw, and string lashing. Let the glue cure according to directions. Then test each joint in three ways: pull apart (tension), twist (torsion), and bend sideways (shear). Rate each joint on strength (1-5 scale for each test), speed of assembly (timed), reversibility (can you take it apart without damage?), skill required (how hard was it?), and cost (estimate material cost). Create a comparison matrix showing all four methods across all criteria. Discuss: which joining method would you choose for a bookshelf? A tent? A picture frame? A temporary display? The answer should be different for each because the constraints are different.
materials: 8 popsicle sticks or small wood pieces (2 per joint)
learning_objectives: Compare four different joining methods across multiple performance criteria
Test joints for strength in tension, torsion, and shear
Understand that the best joining method depends on the application constraints
Use a comparison matrix to evaluate tradeoffs systematically
variations: Foundation (K-2): Try glue and tape only, test by pulling apart, and discuss which was stronger
High School: Add welding or soldering demonstrations (with supervision) and discuss thermal joining versus mechanical joining

name: Simple Casting with Plaster
description: Experience the complete casting process from start to finish. Step 1: Design a simple 3D shape (a medallion, a tile, or a small figure). Step 2: Create a mold by pressing an original shape into modeling clay to form a negative impression, or build a simple open-top mold from cardboard and tape. Step 3: Mix plaster of Paris according to package directions. Step 4: Pour the plaster into the mold, tap gently to release air bubbles, and let it set completely (30-60 minutes). Step 5: Carefully remove the casting from the mold. Step 6: Inspect the casting for defects (air bubbles, incomplete fill, surface roughness). Step 7: Sand or paint the finished casting. Document each step with photos or sketches. Discuss: what shapes are easy to cast and what shapes are difficult? Why? What would you change to get a better result?
materials: cardboard and tape for mold frame
petroleum jelly or cooking spray (mold release)
learning_objectives: Execute a complete casting process: pattern, mold, pour, cure, demold, finish
Understand the relationship between mold design and casting quality
Identify common casting defects and their causes
Recognize casting as a manufacturing process used for mass production
variations: Foundation (K-2): Press objects into playdough to make impressions, observe the negative shape concept
High School: Use two-part molds for more complex shapes, calculate plaster volume needed, and analyze shrinkage

name: Design a Better Lunch Container: Material Selection
description: Design a lunch container that meets these specific requirements: keeps food warm for at least 2 hours, costs under $5 in materials to manufacture, weighs less than 200 grams when empty, is dishwasher safe (withstands 70 degrees Celsius water), and does not leach chemicals into food. Start by listing the material properties each requirement demands (thermal insulation, low cost, low density, heat resistance, food safety). Then identify at least 3 candidate material-process combinations (for example: injection-molded polypropylene, vacuum-formed stainless steel with insulation, thermoformed PET with foam liner). Use a decision matrix to score each option against all requirements (1-5 scale, weighted by importance). Select your final material-process combination and write a one-page justification explaining your choice, including which tradeoffs you made and why.
materials: sample materials for reference (plastic containers, metal containers, foam samples)
learning_objectives: Translate product requirements into material property demands
Identify multiple material-process combinations that could meet requirements
Use a weighted decision matrix to evaluate options systematically
Justify a material-process selection with evidence-based tradeoff reasoning
variations: Elementary: Choose between 2 materials for a simpler container with fewer requirements
High School: Add manufacturing cost modeling (tooling amortization, per-unit cost at different volumes)

name: Cost-Performance Tradeoff Analysis
description: Compare three material options for a bicycle frame: steel (chromoly), aluminum (6061 alloy), and carbon fiber composite. Research and record for each material: weight per frame, tensile strength, cost per frame, repairability (can a damaged frame be fixed?), manufacturing process required, ride quality (vibration damping), lifespan, and environmental impact (energy to produce, recyclability). Create a comparison table and plot key tradeoffs on axes (for example, weight vs. cost, strength vs. repairability). Determine which material is best for three different users: (1) a racing cyclist who wants the lightest frame at any cost, (2) a daily commuter who needs durability and repairability, and (3) a budget-conscious rider who wants the most value. Present your analysis showing that the 'best' material depends entirely on which constraints you prioritize.
materials: computer with internet access for research
learning_objectives: Research and compare real engineering materials across multiple performance dimensions
Create tradeoff visualizations showing that no single material is universally best
Understand that 'best' material depends on user requirements and constraints
Communicate material selection reasoning with data and evidence
variations: Elementary: Compare wood, plastic, and metal for a toy car -- which is strongest, lightest, cheapest?
High School: Add fatigue life analysis and weld versus bonded joint considerations for each material

name: Decomposition Race: Which Material Breaks Down?
description: Bury or compost small samples of 8 different materials: banana peel, paper, cardboard, cotton fabric, plastic bag, aluminum foil, glass bead, and styrofoam piece. Label each burial location clearly. Check weekly for 4-8 weeks, photographing or sketching the condition of each sample. Record observations: has it changed color? Is it softer? Has it shrunk? Is it breaking apart? Has it disappeared? After the observation period, dig up all samples and compare. Create a decomposition timeline showing which materials broke down fastest and which showed no change. Discuss: why do some materials decompose and others do not? Is decomposition always a good thing? When would you want a material that does NOT break down (water pipes, building foundations)? When would you want one that does (packaging, food waste)?
learning_objectives: Observe decomposition rates of different materials through direct experiment
Record observations systematically over multiple weeks
Understand that materials have vastly different decomposition timelines
Recognize that biodegradability is sometimes desirable and sometimes not
variations: Foundation (PreK-K): Bury just 3 items (banana peel, paper, plastic) and check after 2 weeks with adult help
Middle School: Research the actual decomposition timelines for each material and compare to observed results

name: Product Lifecycle Map
description: Choose one common product (a cotton t-shirt, a smartphone, a plastic water bottle, or an aluminum soda can). Research and map its complete material lifecycle across five stages: (1) Raw material extraction -- where do the raw materials come from? What environmental impact does extraction have? (2) Processing -- how are raw materials refined into usable materials? What energy and chemicals are required? (3) Manufacturing -- how is the product assembled? Where is it made? How far does it travel? (4) Use phase -- how long does the product last? Does it require energy or maintenance during use? (5) End-of-life -- what happens when the product is discarded? Can it be recycled, composted, or repaired? Or does it go to landfill? Create a visual lifecycle map (poster, diagram, or digital presentation) showing all five stages with environmental impact estimates at each stage. Identify the highest-impact stage and propose one change that would reduce the total lifecycle impact.
materials: computer with internet access for research
learning_objectives: Trace a complete material lifecycle from extraction through end-of-life
Research and estimate environmental impact at each lifecycle stage
Identify the highest-impact stage in a product lifecycle
Propose evidence-based improvements to reduce total lifecycle environmental impact
variations: Elementary: Map the lifecycle of a simple product (paper, wooden pencil) with guided questions at each stage
High School: Use quantitative lifecycle analysis data (carbon footprint, water use, energy) and compare two competing products
